questions.question: 1 (a) Common wombats mark areas of their habitat to signal to other wombats of the same species that they are living there. These marked areas are called territories. Common wombats produce cube-shaped faeces. It is thought that these cubes can be stacked to mark territory without rolling away. (i) Calculate the surface area : volume ratio of one cube with a length of 2.5 cm. (2) Answer ..
questions.text: 1 (a) Common wombats mark areas of their habitat to signal to other wombats of the same species that they are living there. These marked areas are called territories. Common wombats produce cube-shaped faeces. It is thought that these cubes can be stacked to mark territory without rolling away. (i) Calculate the surface area : volume ratio of one cube with a length of 2.5 cm. (2) Answer ..

parts.answer: An explanation that includes the following points:  because of water uptake / increased {volume of cytoplasm / size of vacuole} (1)  (increase in size due to) {synthesis/production} of {organelles / (named) proteins / enzymes} (1)  therefore {synthesis/production} of {new/more} cell {membrane / wall} (1) ACCEPT organelles increase in size (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark  calculation of surface area and volume (1)  correct ratio (1) Example of calculation: surface area = 6 × 2.5 = 37.5 volume = 2.5 = 15.625 2.4:1 ECF applies Correct answer with no working shown scores full marks (2) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes the following point:  place where {organisms / species} live (1) (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes the following point:  organisms that can reproduc e with others to form fertile offspring (1) (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

An answer that includes one of the following points:  (inheritance of) {many/multip le/several/different} genes {contribute to / code for} height (of wombat) (1)  genes at different loci {contribute to / code for} height (of wombat) (1) Accept different positions on a chromosome (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

A description that includes two of the following points:  {long/sharp} claws / large paws (for digging) (1)  {short / powerful} legs (for digging) (1)  ears face backwards / small eyes (so don’t fill with soil) (1) ACCEPT big feet / long nails ACCEPT short ears / large pupils (2) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes one of the following points:  (analysis of/ comparison of/ similarities in / differences in) {DNA / RNA / proteins / amino acid sequences / enzymes / ribosomes / membrane components / cell wall components} (1) ACCEPT patterns in non-coding DNA ACCEPT genes ignore organelles (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark A description that includes th ree of the following points:  peer review (1)  repetition of experiments (by other scientists to see if same data are collected) (1)  {analysis / evaluation / discussion / comparison} of data (1) ACCEPT extend the analysis e.g. look for {similarities / differences} in more genes (1) (2) Question Number Mark

one mark for each correct row Feature Archaea only Archaea and Bacteria only Archaea and Eukarya only Archaea and Bacteria and Eukarya absence of a nuclear envelope  presence of circular DNA  presence of a cell membrane  presence of ribosomes  (4) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

An answer that includes two of the following points:  minerals (1)  (suitable) pH (1)  (suitable) light (intensity) (1) ACCEPT named mineral e.g. phosphate, nitrates ACCEPT oxygen, nitrogen, carbon dioxide, water ignore nutrients / moisture / predation / glucose / temperature ACCEPT suitable wavelength of light / sunlight (2) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is D William Withering A is not correct beca use Withering tested his digitalis soup B is not correct because Withering tested his digitalis soup C is not correct because Withering tested his digitalis soup (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An explanation that includes the following points:  patients given a range of {doses / concentrations} / doses increase in concentration (throughout the investigation) (1)  (to allow selection of) lowest effective concentration / highest dose that can be tolerated without serious side effects (1) ACCEPT (selection of) dose that is effective with minimal side effects (2) Question Number Additional guidance Mark A description that includes the following points:  (selection of) patients with (colon / kidney) cancer (1)  {half / one group} given {curacin A / (this) cancer drug} and {half / one group} given current (best) drug (1)  neither doctor nor patient knows which treatment has been given (1)  analysis of data to see if curacin A is more effective than the {current best drug / placebo} (1) ignore ref to stage of trial ACCEPT placebo / sugar pill ACCEPT analysis to see if there is a {significant difference/improvement} between those who took the drug compared to the placebo (4) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

An answer that includes three of the following points:  deforestation / loss of habitat (1)  increased grazing (of forest) (1)  reduced number of new trees (1)  disease (that has killed the trees) (1)  reduced genetic diversity (1) ACCEPT loss of trees due to natural events e.g. landslides / tsunamis / fire / poor soil quality ACCEPT new grazing species e.g. reduced {pollination / reproduction / pollinators} / tree doesn’t flower often / reduced enzyme activity due to {changing temperatures / drought} / few trees producing seeds / {air pollution / global warming / climate change} e.g. fungal disease ACCEPT reproduce asexually so no variation (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark  correct line measurement and conversion (1)  correct calculation (1)  correct answer in standard form (1) ACCEPT ±1mm Example of calculation: 000μm (38 000 ÷ 200) =190 (μm) 1.9 × 10 (μm) ecf applies Correct answer with no working shown scores full marks (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes the following points:  {transport/ translocation} of {sucrose / amino acids} (1)  from {source /site of production / leaves} to {sink / site of use / site of storage} (1) ACCEPT products of photosynthesis / lipids / hormones / mRNA / sugars ignore glucose (2) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is C two A is not correct because all the statements are correct apart from, they do not contain cytoplasm B is not correct because all the statements are correct apart from, they do not contain cytoplasm D is not correct because they do not contain cytoplasm (1) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

An answer that includes three of the following points:  the edge of the xylem is closer to the {outside of the structure / epidermis} (than the phloem) in the root / the phloem is closer to the {outside of the structure / epidermis} than the xylem in the stem (1)  xylem and phloem are contained in (separate vascular) bundles in the stem (1)  more equal phloem:xylem in stem (whereas 4:1 in root)  (xylem and phloem / (vascular) bundles) are arranged in {outer part of / a circular pattern in} stem (1) ACCEPT xylem and phloem are separated by cambium in stem ignore sclerenchyma/parenchyma ACCEPT xylem and phloem together make an oval shape ignore {sizes/number unqualified} of {xylem/phloem} ACCEPT both xylem and phloem are closer to epidermis in stem ACCEPT (xylem and phloem/ (vascular) bundles) are arranged in all areas of stem (in monocots) ACCEPT vascular tissue in one area in the root whereas in several areas in the stem ACCEPT labelled diagram showing the distribution in the stem (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An explanation that includes three of the following points:  {polymer of / contains} glucose because (glucose) is an energy source (1)  {branched / 1,6 bonds} theref ore can be rapidly hydrolysed (to release glucose/energy) (1)  compact so more {glucose/energy} can be stored / compact so high energy density} (1)  insoluble therefore does not affect osmosis (1) ACCEPT branched so quickly broken down ignore easy to break down ACCEPT compact so does not take up much space ACCEPT too big to diffuse across cell surface membrane / large molecules therefore remain in cells (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes the following point:  amyloplast (1) (1) Question Number Mark The only correct answer is D 67 % A is not correct because that is 100-67 B is not correct because the correct answer is 67 % C is not correct because that is incorrectly rounded from 66.6667 (1) Question Number Additional parts.text: 7 (b) The table shows some features of living organisms. Which box in each row shows whether the feature is found in these domains? (4) FeatureArchaea only Archaea and Bacteria only Archaea and Eukarya only Archaea and Bacteria and Eukarya absence of a nuclear envelope presence of circular DNA presence of a cell membrane presence of ribosomes (Total for Question 3 = 7 marks) *P6710
parts.answer: An answer that includes the following points:  histone modification can be passed onto zygotes (1)  if enzyme M is present, hi stone modification is passed on to {all chromosomes / embryo} (after cell division in experiment 1) (1)  cells divide by mitosis (1)  if enzyme M is absent (in the egg cell cytoplasm) then the histone modification decreases until no longer detected (1)  (histone modification decreases because) replicated DNA can’t be modified without enzyme (M) (1)  description of histone modification (1) ACCEPT fertilised egg cells (in both experiments) have histone modification ACCEPT enzyme M causes histone modification ACCEPT histone modification is passed on to {all chromosomes/embryo} in experiment 1 ACCEPT cell division involves replication of DNA ACCEPT histone modification is not passed on to all chromosomes in experiment 2 ACCEPT enzyme M can’t be produced (if absent in egg cell) ignore genes being switched {on/off} (5) Question Number Additional guidance Mark

An explanation that includes two of the following points:  haploid so that when it is fertilised {a zygote is formed / it becomes diploid} (1)  contains lipid droplets as a source of energy (1)  {cortical granules / enzymes} to {harden zona pellucida / prevent polyspermy} (1) ACCEPT large cell to store more {lipid/energy} ACCEPT releases chemicals to attract sperm ACCEPT sperm bind to glycoproteins on egg cell (surface) (2) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An answer that includes the following points: Similarities  lining up of {chromosomes/ chromatids} on equator (of cell) (1)  centromere bonds to spindle fibres (in metaphase) (1) Differences (max 2):  two metaphase stages in meioisis whereas mitosis has one (1)  {independent/random} assortment occurs (in metaphase I) in meiosis whereas it does not occur in mitosis (1)  meiosis involves homologous pairs of chromosomes (in metaphase I) and sister chromatids (in metaphase II) whereas mitosis involves sister chromatids (1) Full marks can only be awarded if there is a similarity in the answer ACCEPT centre of cell / metaphase plate ACCEPT {chromosomes/chromatids} bond to spindle fibres ACCEPT independent assortment only occurs in metaphase I in meiosis ACCEPT meiosis involves pairs of chromosomes whereas mitosis involves pairs of {sister chromatids/ single chromosomes} ACCEPT meiosis involves pairs of chromosomes whereas mitosis does not (3) Question Number Additional guidance Mark An explanation that includes four of the following points:  morula cells are totipotent whereas blastocyst cells are pluripotent (1)  (specialisation / differentiat ion occurs because) gene(s) switched off (in blastocyst cells) (1)  due to {epigenetic modification / DNA methylation / histone modification} (1)  transcription of (active) genes to produce (active) mRNA / (active) mRNA translated at the ribosomes (1)  the protein(s) produced {determine / change} cell {structure / function} (1) ACCEPT cells become pluripotent
